history: React (component-based, large ecosystem, quick prototyping) → Node.js/Express with third-party dining/campus API integration (e.g., HotSpot, Tapingo)
description: The project builds a React-based web application that aggregates real-time dining hall data from third-party campus APIs (via a Node.js/Express backend) to let students search, filter, and compare dining options by location, hours, and wait times. The core feature is a dynamic dashboard that updates wait-time estimates and menu availability throughout the day, allowing students to plan meal timing and choose between dining halls. The two scenarios compare a student planning ahead using historical menu data versus one making a same-day decision based on current wait times and live inventory.
consultant_notes: React + Node is a sound tool choice for web prototyping and fits the 14-day window comfortably. The plan's main weakness is scope ambiguity: 'real-time wait times' and 'external API integration' are placeholders without commitment to a specific data source or fallback strategy. The conceptual challenge is moderate—this is a straightforward CRUD + filtering app without emergent dynamics or deep systems complexity. For a 14-day constraint, the team has breathing room, but the plan risks either becoming too narrow (basic menu browser) or running into friction from API unavailability mid-project.
annotator_notes: Real-time data would require building out those campus APIs as well, or finding previous projects that worked on this idea and connecting with those developers. Difficult for a 14 day project.

history: React (component-based, large ecosystem, quick prototyping) → Node.js/Express server with scraped dining hall data (HTML parsing or Playwright)
description: The student is building a React web app that pulls dining hall information (menus, hours, wait times) from campus sources using a Node.js backend that scrapes and updates the data regularly. The app's core feature is a filterable search interface where students can view available dining locations, current operating status, and meal options in real time. The two scenarios being compared would likely be comparing the user experience of browsing by location versus browsing by meal type, or contrasting peak dining times against off-peak availability.
consultant_notes: React + Node.js is a sound, justifiable tech stack for 14 days, and web app prototyping sits comfortably within feasibility. The scope is well-defined (3+ features, external data, design doc, demo), but the plan lacks conceptual depth—this is competent full-stack work, not systems modeling or emergent behavior analysis. The real risk is scope creep in feature polish (responsive design, authentication, error handling) eating the 14-day window; the plan would benefit from explicit prioritization and a clear 'MVP definition' to protect time for the design document and thoughtful data architecture decisions.
annotator_notes: Achievable but somewhat easier to implement plan. Would also teach the students the basics of CRUD apps. React is also more popular in the industry right now.

history: Vue (lighter learning curve, good for rapid development) → Serverless backend (Firebase/Supabase) with pre-loaded or real-time dining data
description: This project uses a Vue.js frontend with a serverless Firebase backend to build a campus dining finder app where students can search dining halls, filter by menu items or hours, and see current wait times. The core feature is a real-time data sync system that updates wait times and menu availability as students check in, creating a live picture of which dining spots are busy. The two scenarios are distinguished by whether wait times are manually updated by dining staff (scheduled data mode) or pulled from anonymous student check-ins (crowdsourced real-time mode).
consultant_notes: Vue + serverless is a sound, feasible choice that avoids the setup friction of traditional full-stack frameworks—good alignment with a 2-week deadline. However, the plan needs sharper specificity on the data source (scraped vs. mocked?) and emergence of features; as written, it risks being a straightforward CRUD app rather than one that surfaces non-obvious dining insights (e.g., demand prediction, group coordination). The design doc and AI disclosure are explicitly required and non-negotiable.
annotator_notes: Cool idea. The real-time data sync can be implemented in these 14 days and is a stand-out feature.

history: Plain HTML/CSS/JS (minimal dependencies, simpler deployment) → Public dining API if available (most reliable, but may not exist for target campus)
description: This project builds a web app using vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript that aggregates campus dining information and displays it with search and filter capabilities for students planning meals. The core mechanism is a client-side data layer that either pulls from a public dining API or falls back to manually maintained JSON files, allowing the app to function regardless of whether the campus provides an official data source. The two scenarios differ in data freshness and maintenance burden: the API scenario delivers real-time information but depends on external infrastructure, while the manual data scenario requires periodic updates but gives full control over what students see.
consultant_notes: Plain HTML/CSS/JS is a defensible choice for a 14-day web app, but risks underbaked architecture if you're building real filtering/search logic from scratch—vanilla JS can get messy fast without a framework scaffold. The dining API dependency is a real blocker: most campuses don't expose public APIs, so you should front-load a feasibility spike (day 1–2) to confirm data access; if you fall back to scraping or mocked data, the architecture story becomes weaker and the 'real-time' requirement gets harder to justify credibly.
annotator_notes: Too minimal - students would not pick up skills for app development frameworks. Also the APIs would likely not be available. Worst combination: wrong framework and uncertain data source.

history: Plain HTML/CSS/JS (minimal dependencies, simpler deployment) → Mock/hardcoded dataset (full control, no external dependencies, faster development)
description: This project builds a web application using vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript that aggregates campus dining information from a hardcoded dataset, allowing students to search and filter dining halls by location, menu items, and operating hours. The core feature is a dynamic filtering system where students can view real-time wait times and meal plans across multiple dining locations, with the two scenarios distinguished by whether a student searches by specific dietary preferences (vegetarian options available now) versus browsing all open dining halls at their current time of day.
consultant_notes: Plain HTML/CSS/JS is a defensible choice for a 14-day web app prototype and avoids framework overhead, but hardcoding the dataset misses a key learning objective: integrating an external data source. The plan is feasible and well-scoped for the timeline, but loses points for sidestepping the 'at least one external data source' requirement rather than meeting it thoughtfully. Consider: fetch from a real dining API (even if mocked server-side), or parse a CSV/JSON file dynamically—both stay simple while demonstrating data integration, which is half the technical substance of a dining app.
annotator_notes: Too simple and relying on hardcoded data. Not enough for a 14 day project.
